title: Unit 9 Checkpoint Quiz — Formal Writing & Email

questions.prompt: Which is the correct format for a formal salutation in a Mexican professional email?
questions.explanation: Formal Mexican emails use 'Estimado/a' + title + last name + colon. The colon (not comma) is standard in Spanish formal correspondence. 'Querido' is too personal.

questions.prompt: What does 'Me dirijo a usted para' signal in an email?
options: The purpose of the email — 'I am writing to you to'
questions.explanation: 'Me dirijo a usted para...' = I am writing to you to... It's the standard formal opening that states the purpose of the email upfront.

questions.prompt: Which is the most common formal email closing in Mexico?
questions.explanation: 'Atentamente' (sincerely/attentively) is the standard formal closing. 'Saludos' is semi-formal. 'Cuídate' and 'Un abrazo' are personal and inappropriate for professional correspondence.

questions.prompt: In Mexico, the title 'Lic.' is used for:
questions.explanation: 'Lic.' (Licenciado/a) in Mexico refers to anyone holding a university degree — it's used much more broadly than in other countries. Not using it when addressing someone who holds it is considered disrespectful.

questions.prompt: Which phrase makes a polite formal request?
options: Le agradecería que me enviara la información.
questions.explanation: 'Le agradecería que...' (I would be grateful if you...) uses the conditional for politeness + subjunctive for the request. It's the gold standard for formal written requests in Mexican Spanish.

questions.prompt: What is 'asimismo' used for in formal writing?
questions.explanation: 'Asimismo' = likewise / additionally. It's a formal connector used to add another point or request in the same email, without using informal words like 'también' or 'y'.

questions.prompt: What does 'quedo a sus órdenes' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Quedo a sus órdenes' = I remain at your service — a formal expression of availability and helpfulness used as a closing statement in Mexican professional correspondence.

questions.prompt: Why do Mexican professional emails often begin with a brief pleasantry before the main point?
options: Mexican professional culture values warmth — jumping straight to the request can seem brusque
It is required by Mexican law
It makes the email longer, which looks more professional
It is the only grammatically correct way to open an email
questions.explanation: Mexican professional culture places high value on warmth and relationship-building. A brief pleasantry before the main request shows respect and consideration. A blunt, direct opening ('I need X') without any warm opening can be perceived as rude.
